speaker: interviewer
text: We are interviewing Mr. Godel Silber. And this is tape number two. Can we go back a little bit? Who was with you in the cattle car?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother, brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Esther-- maiden name, Schuter-- S-C-H-U-T-E-R. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And who else, Mr. Silber?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: And his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Meir Isaac Silber.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Would you like to continue? That you got to Birkenau in the middle of the night, and Mengele made his selection.

speaker: interviewee
text: [SOB] Mother and brother-- took away mother right away-- didn't see her never-- and Meir Isaac. And I had another brother. He just died too. It was Leon Silber. He come. So Leon Silber, and me, and Meir Isaac, we all come to Birkenau. His wife-- them two wives-- they were married to the-- the two wives went away. I didn't see them no more. My brother who has just died, he saw-- he saw his wife. And when he saw his wife to the-- through the fence-- and I never saw once inside. They never saw. Meir Isaac never saw his wife. She was pregnant. Pregnant ladies never took to the camp with children-- never. He never saw his wife when we come to Birkenau. He lived up to Shabbat HaGadol, you know, a-- a week before like a-- a week before Pesach. He died in the camp. The first Jew who died who was hiding-- was hidden, I mean, on to-- into the-- onto the bed-- not in the bed-- you know-- what they had.

speaker: interviewer
text: The boards.

speaker: interviewee
text: What?

speaker: interviewer
text: The boards?

speaker: interviewee
text: Under the board, that's right. He died Shabbat HaGadol.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, OK. And they made the selection. And then what happened? Who took you, and where-- where did they take you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Take? To Birkenau, in the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: In-- in the camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the camp, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the camp, they keep us till early morning. Early morning, the kapo, right away, they hit you. He was awful. He was not normal. He wasn't a human being. Forget about human being.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then we thought-- I said, how can it be-- could-- could-- could that happen, one Jew to the another one? See, like Auschwitz was Polish, more Polish. Birkenau was more Jewish.

speaker: interviewee
text: Of Auschwitz he was a little human being. I don't mean the people, I mean the-- there was over there like a old-- old-- in there are-- the Polish army had over there the barracks. And here we-- we built it ourselves in Birkenau-- old-- old-- very old nothing. Auschwitz we had water. We had the toilet. In Birkenau, no toilets, no water, no nothing.

speaker: interviewee
text: So early in the morning, we had to get up 4 o'clock on the morning. We go to work-- go to work, to what's happen-- bickering with a swarm. I don't know if you heard about it-- with-- with-- with mosquitoes.

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- they choose it. I think it was a few hundred mile. He choose that around, because it was not just vacant. It was lots of camps around. In every camp there it was a few-- a few thousand, many thousand-- 15,000, 30,000. Because the-- the-- the [NON-ENGLISH] had a coal mine and salt mine around over there. So that's why they needed the people to work over there.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we come to Birkenau early in the morning, put ourselves on the line. We should stay five in the front like that-- a long line. So we stay a long line. And he said, OK. You go here. You go here. It's already with the kapo side and over to here.

speaker: interviewee
text: They took us to work. So we going to work. See, if they took a piece of land-- I remember like that, nothing. That was when we built the crematorium. We start to build a-- when I come, there was no crematorium yet. There was a crematorium in Auschwitz-- when you died, a few people-- 50 or 100.

speaker: interviewee
text: Over here was a mass, mass, mass thing. So hardly people could, they couldn't get-- so from Birkenau, right from the ramp, they took in big trucks. And it was a big pit or hole-- pit or hole-- very big. And the ram, like a-- what do you call it? The-- from the lamps-- it used to be wooden when you hold there-- the telephone or the [NON-ENGLISH]. Poles.

speaker: interviewee
text: The poles. So they were-- see, I'm talking with the hand too, a little. [LAUGHS] They took-- they took-- they put a ramp there-- the pit or hole. And they back up with the diesel truck mats. The people were on the top. They dumped them alive to that-- for that-- that pit, Judy, it was 24 hours-- never went out. The smoke of your life-- the scream you could hear. I wouldn't say in Krakow. The smoke in Krakow you could have seen nighttime. So that-- that-- that was the life.

speaker: interviewee
text: So right away we said, well, what-- what we got-- we got to lose? So we looked around. What we can do? In every day, every day we walked out like some 200. We come back under 30, because the kapo killed them.

speaker: interviewee
text: If you killed a man, the portion bread whether you have to give him, you don't have to give him anymore. So he killed him, that he should have that portion bread.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what-- when was this? What-- when was the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what time of the year it was?

speaker: interviewer
text: What time of the year? Was it in--

speaker: interviewee
text: November.

speaker: interviewer
text: In November.

speaker: interviewee
text: November I would say-- the 27-- 27.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was very cold.

speaker: interviewee
text: It were cold already, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how-- how were you dressed? Did--

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] Oh, well.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, did they--

speaker: interviewee
text: They give you. What they gave you? Let's see. They give you-- like, I-- I was not so lucky after. They gave me one shoe, a big one like that, you know-- and a ladies shoe. So how can I-- if I need a size 12, they gave you size 6. You were lucky if it's somebody-- you couldn't talk with nobody, because right away, they hit you. Mostly people got killed. At that time was no crematorium-- with the handles, shovel handles. They hit you over the head. You fall down. They put the shovel-- the handle here. One fellow stay here and then another fellow stay here-- like that. That was-- that was the German culture.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they gave you a uniform?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not in Birkenau. Birkenau, anything-- but they fight. Because before we come, they killed about 12,000 Russian to have their suits. So they give us. In Auschwitz, when I come, like it were done a few days later-- I would say four days later-- he come and-- and called name-- me, Godel Silber. And-- no, no. I'm sorry, no names-- by the number already-- they gave us numbers.

speaker: interviewer
text: You already received your numbers?

speaker: interviewee
text: The numbers. That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your number?

speaker: interviewee
text: 7 9-- 73978. We-- I-- we had six-- six numbers together-- me, and my two brothers, another three brothers-- not Silber-- Zabludowicz, with a Z. They all still alive in-- in Israel. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Continue.

speaker: interviewee
text: So when we-- and a few days later, they called us by the number, and he said whether we-- if he called our number, we should never go to work. Call out by the number, so I see the fellow, Zabludowicz, he-- he like, when I come to tattoo myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they told me what profession you had? So lots of people think, OK, I'm gonna say a chauffeur-- a chauffeur-- chauffeur-- a driver. So who's gonna [LAUGHS] give you a truck in Auschwitz to drive there anyway? I said, I don't know what's happening. I said, mechanic. So they called off my name. They said, you're a mechanic? I said, yes. And Zabludowicz, electrician-- all three brothers, electrician. And my brother said he's a barber in a [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewee
text: So-- so he stayed in Birkenau with another brother, and me, and another maybe, I would say, 15 or 20. Another block they took again, you know, from-- then another block-- another barracks, I mean. Block is a barrack. And they gave us branches to hold-- big branches. So we said, he didn't know. So everybody says-- oh, yeah. He start to cry. Godel, don't forget me. Don't forget what's happened. You're gonna be alive. You what-- work like a mechanic over there. And the-- the SS, they didn't want to know where we going, so they give us big branches to hold it. They give us big the branches to hold it. And we hold it. And we walked to Auschwitz. That took us maybe a 1/2 an hour-- take about 1/2 an hour from Birkenau to Auschwitz. We didn't know. We come before. We stayed. They don't let us in right away. And I see a sign on the top the gate-- Arbeit Macht Frei-- you know, on the top the gate-- Arbeit Macht Frei.

speaker: interviewee
text: So that mean, OK, I'm gonna work here three months, six month. And I'm gonna be released, yes. So they got us inside.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, but everybody was scared, because Birken-Auschwitz was very clean. If you had louse, they kill you. In Birkenau, it was billions and billions.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you translate what was written on the gate of Auschwitz--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --into English?

speaker: interviewee
text: Arbeit-- you know, the work release you. Arbeit macht frei.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. I'm sorry. Continue.

speaker: interviewee
text: The working. Arbeit is working.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: If you work, you're gonna be released. It's still there. I think it's in the book too. In every camp, they put that up at that sign.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you got to Auschwitz,

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did they do with you when you-- you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Wait a minute. That what I said.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] We got to Auschwitz. They-- they didn't want to know-- nobody wants to walk close to us. So before we got inside in-- in the camp, we dumped the-- the branches so that-- we couldn't see. They were walking. We couldn't see nothing, because lots of people went back and forth with worker-- civil-- that way we shouldn't talk to them. And we-- they shouldn't see us either.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we got over there, some-- some-- some SS come out, and some workers come out to see what's happen, what's going on with the-- what do you call it? The special shorts, the long blue and white-- you ever saw them-- them shorts? The uniforms?

speaker: interviewee
text: The uniforms, yeah-- blue and white. So I saw workers. I said, what's going on? We come from over there. No human being here. At least you can see it. He's cut nice, shaved, shoes.

speaker: interviewee
text: So all of a sudden, a transfer, that they were-- they-- they were the same like me. Maybe they come a few days before. And-- and they took us to the zone. And before we went into the zone, they told us everything-- everybody naked. You should never keep even a hankie-- nothing. We didn't have nothing, because everything was so-- so much lice. I don't know where it's going. And we got into the zone. You know what the zone is? Tell us what is it.

speaker: interviewee
text: The zone is in a place where it's-- where-- disinfection. And there's hot water-- take the shower. We got in over there. We got in over there. We stand on a stool. Women, men-- it don't make any difference-- all together. You stand on the stool, and they start to shave here, under the arm-- and start to shave here. But we don't [? have ?] [? razor. ?] [LAUGHS] Nobody got that. We don't have razor. But I was so sore, you would never believe it. They ripped it out. And then after, they give some-- I don't know what was it-- Naphtha-- to put it on here-- disinfection. And after this, we-- we went to-- to the shower. We got nothing to eat. It's already Thursday-- nothing to eat. When we went over there, we have to walk out. So the-- the zone was so big. They only took 20 people.

speaker: interviewee
text: Where is everybody going? So they took 20 people when it's cold already, Judy. So you're going out. You have to stay and wait for the dress. So it freezes everything. [LAUGHS] They didn't give you nothing. By the time we got on the block, it was maybe 12 hours later.

speaker: interviewee
text: We got over to the block. They show us-- listen-- different story entirely. Everybody got the-- his bunk-- a bunk from separate-- not 10, 12 together-- everybody. It was 1, 2, 3. So I had the middle one, because Zabludowicz. And so his brothers went in another one-- not that he went-- they took-- where they took him. But we never lost contact. So when we went over there, a difference entirely. You're human being. You go downstair, you got a toilet already-- like here, in mine house-- hot water and cold water-- a sink. We had to go out every morning and wash yourself. But God forbid, if you find a louse. Then it was finished.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know you have to have a commander to go to work. So they thought I'm mechanic even, and gave me like a mechanic to work. Oh, but listen. The Polish took the best jobs. He had to do the daily work too. So the daily work, they-- they give to the Jewish. So-- so how long could you hold up? Outside-- [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] Continue, Mr. Silber.

speaker: interviewee
text: Outside, how long could you-- could you hold up? It was so cold. The first day they could-- they gave me-- when I come alone, a mechanic, they give me-- and the commander for mechanic.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I go over there. It's a different story. The inside, it's warm.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It's warm, you know. So I said, listen. If that's the case, we gonna survive. I was talking to myself and to my friends. We survived.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we came home. Next morning-- no, no-- the same night, the fellow who-- who marked down that I should go to-- to a mechanic, he told me, don't come tomorrow. If you come tomorrow here, they gonna kill you.

speaker: interviewee
text: He brought you here because somebody was sick. Another fellow was sick. The fellow was sick. Come back-- no room for you.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I said, listen. Better one day like nothing. So I was over there. I was over there. And I went away.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was scared. I was scared already to go back. So I went over there. And I was away at least for 10 days-- no, maybe not-- for 10 days. And I said I cannot hold on happen. My back doesn't hold me anymore. I couldn't hold out the back.

speaker: interviewee
text: So because you had to work outside, and go with stones, it was not much to work. Stones, bring it here and then bring-- go back with the stone. So after this, I don't care if I die or not or if they kill me. If I-- if I at least have-- have another day, a good day. If I go from the-- from the-- from the gate, they can't take me back.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a commander, what they called him, Moll-- and-- when they make the airplanes, and done with and their way-- [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewee
text: So that was-- I get a really good now, you know, from them So I come over there. The fellow-- can't-- can't-- he say one-- one fellow is too much here. Because I had that in my block with 50 now. And the-- and the work commander was 17-- the [NON-ENGLISH] where the-- [NON-ENGLISH] is when you build, and take stones, like that. So I said-- I lift up my hand. I am here. He said, listen. You know you're not supposed to come here. So what are you doing here? Where were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, I was in-- in hospital, like in-- in the revere. It was-- it was not such a thing a Jew could have-- won't come back home. If a Jew went over there, forget about, Judy. The Pollacks never let them out. This is 99% of Polish doctors-- some Jewish doctors maybe. They were in a Polish name.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I say-- he said listen. They let you out? I says, yes. OK. Sorry, I was sick.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the meantime, stay here. I don't know what to do. So I stayed over there, and they give me to-- to a [NON-ENGLISH], like a [NON-ENGLISH]. It was [NON-ENGLISH] were, it was blacksmith. It was welders. I had a [NON-ENGLISH]. We were-- we were 240, 250.

speaker: interviewer
text: People?

speaker: interviewee
text: People and that commander. When I was-- and I was-- soon we come in. I was the first. I was close to a lot of blacksmith and everything-- everybody. They-- they-- they know me and everything.

speaker: interviewee
text: So soon I come in. And I say, I talked and talked a little bit. It's a wheel man comes in. And he goes to the oberkapo, to mine oberkapo. I know the-- I know even the number. He had 42-- 40-- no-- 27042.

speaker: interviewee
text: He's alive. He was a very good man. And another one was 27041-- one number different. So when I come over there, that wheel man goes over to that oberman. And he said, listen. I need some chains-- to make chains. He said, listen. I don't do nothing. That he-- he told the-- the to the civil, that oberkapo. We don't do that. So-- so I told-- I mixed in a little bit. I said, and he shouldn't do that. I said, listen. I know how to do it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so I says, if he give me the chain, you got to give me the material on a snow chain. You know, it used to be-- not your time. It used to be here snow chain too. You remember the snow chain? You ever saw a snow chain? No. You know, the cars with lots of snow in Canada. Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was no snow tires here. There was snow chain. So if that's the case, he said, you can do it? I says, yes. I won't think I wouldn't be able to. I need the locks. Because if you put them on on the wheels, I make them loose. And then I have to have locks, that the chain should be tight on the wheels-- like that-- turns the wheel and the chain, and they cross-- cross like that-- the chain-- here like that, and here one chain.

speaker: interviewee
text: So in the meantime, he brought-- the-- the civil-- the chain. He brought the chain. He brought it right away [LAUGHS] all right. That was a million dollar. I were-- I almost said, if that's the case, I can make the chains in one day, because tomorrow it might be throw me out. So I told the civilian. He said, listen. That has to take me longer. Because of the-- the locks, I can make it like that. I have to make everything. So he went to that oberkapo and told the oberkapo, listen. That man knows what he's doing. So that-- that where I was staying already. And he brought every day a loaf of bread.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I was supposed to make it in two weeks. So he comes one day. I said, I had a little bit. He says, listen. You gonna make me the chain? Is it gonna be in summer?

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, no, no. Listen. He said to me, I know it. You want the bread. I said, no. It's not the point, the bread. Sure, though, the bread is very important. And over there, when I was over there, I could have a hot water too. I made myself a-- from of piece of pipe-- a-- a kettle-- you know, well done on the bottom-- the-- underneath the bottom.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, how you cook? You can't see the steam. It's no good. Where you gonna cook? So I took a piece of steel, heavy steel. Let's assume I had-- as a big piece like that plate-- the left plate-- very heavy. And I put that over there with the blacksmith, you know, where to cook. Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we heat that up. It's like you got to have plate.

speaker: interviewer
text: I understand.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, you don't have to have.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were able to get some--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: --hot water?

speaker: interviewee
text: Water, yeah, but they know as soon as I said--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: --it was water. So I've got to have a water-- this. And I brought over-- heat up a piece, still like that. And you put that in a pail. You put this on the top, and that boil it up.

speaker: interviewer
text: Make-- so how you gonna make coffee? So we had to have for fuel, water, peeling-- peeling from potatoes. And Bernard, he made coffee better like Sanka.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: We had-- Can you tell us about the routine, like when-- in Auschwitz, when you woke up in the morning, did you have to stand? For instance--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --in roll call? For how long? How long did you stand?

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- I got up 4 o'clock in the morning everyday. We had to stay at least two hours-- two hours for sure-- if they were good. The-- the-- to work was not so bad-- that the-- way on to home-- two hours for sure to stay-- cold, rain, snow, no-- no problem. Sometimes we freeze stiff.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long-- were you able to be a mechanic for the whole time you were in Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did you stay in Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: I stay in Auschwitz to 1945-- no, '44-- when we blew up the crematorium.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you part of the blowing up of the crematorium?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not part-- I was the-- the head of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you like to talk about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I was the head of it. I'm not scared. You can put up my name-- Godel Silber was the head of the crematorium to blow up, because it's in book. I got the book. I don't know if you saw that. I'm gonna get another one, though from [NON-ENGLISH]. Did you hear [NON-ENGLISH]. He's over there.

speaker: interviewer
text: [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH]. Yeah. He died in 19-- I getting another one, though. He died in-- I got his books here anyway-- in 1960 something. But his wife finish it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he was with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. [NON-ENGLISH] was there. He was-- what-- read the book. He write the books--

speaker: interviewer
text: He wrote.

speaker: interviewee
text: --like you.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: Like she.

speaker: interviewer
text: So tell us-- tell us, though. How was it planned? When did you do it?

speaker: interviewee
text: [CRYING]

speaker: interviewer
text: How many of you were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- I didn't know you. You didn't know me. We didn't know. It could be my brother. My brother didn't know what I'm doing. You gonna go to Windsor. You die ahead. I'll give you an-- a man. You go over there. But I keep my ammunition. He keep mine ammunition, that man-- still alive-- 90 till-- over 90-- 90 years old. I'll give you the name. He lives in Windsor.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: His son is a rabbi in the shul.

speaker: interviewer
text: Continue. Tell me, where was this idea conceived to blow up the crematorium?

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't have nothing to lose!

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a meeting? How could you talk to each other? How--

speaker: interviewee
text: Listen. The same thing-- I-- we talked by hand. What could we lose? You see, every day so many people. There was the Hungarian come-- and go like sheep, they said. They didn't go like sheep, believe me. They had no choice. Like I walked through. I could have gone in-- in the-- I went in crematorium every day. I was in crematorium every day like a mechanic. I was every day in crematorium. I know that Otto Moll who-- who burned the people. He was--

speaker: interviewer
text: What--

speaker: interviewee
text: --the head-- the head man.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you repeat his name please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Moll. Moll.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: He after-- he come to [NON-ENGLISH], and they opened a camp in his name-- Moll. [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So what we had to lose? And then you don't even mention there used to be a girl. I don't know. You should know history. You know history of the-- she was-- she was Polish. But she lived in Sweden.

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I took her out from-- I could have got her away from Auschwitz every day. I had that-- I had that suit, SS suit, and I had a gun. And I had the papers-- everything. That fellow in Windsor. He keep that for me.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get that?

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] That's another story. As soon I come, I was with a fellow, but the-- they hanged him in Birkenau. He had ran away with that girl. I give him-- I-- I took him out. His name was Edek Galinski. His number was-- he was from the first. When he come with no shoes-- nothing-- he had-- he was going to the same school like Pope John here-- then the pope from-- from-- from Rome, he went with him to school together. And how I got it, you wondered. That fellow, Edek Galinski, he-- we worked together like a mechanic, the same-- whatever he did-- don't make a difference, whatever-- he was Pole. This, and he was a student like a [NON-ENGLISH] they called it. He was a student. And he respect-- he was a old number. Because maybe 10 people from East Frankfort was alive. The rest, everything was finished. So the inspector, me neither, had nothing to do.

speaker: interviewee
text: So when we went to-- I went after for a year to Auschwitz-- to Birkenau. I was not always in Auschwitz. I stay in Birkenau another year before I got-- before I-- when I could blow the crematoriums up, then I-- I ran away from Birkenau. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Tell me though, what-- didn't they try and kill you? How did you get away?

speaker: interviewee
text: Who? Who tried?

speaker: interviewer
text: The Germans, after, in-- in the trying of the blowing up of the crematorium?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nobody know! [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: They didn't know?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I tell you, my brother didn't know. That fellow in Windsor, he knew it. My wife knew it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what-- when was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in 1944. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And it just happened. And-- and-- and you went back to your-- to-- to your lager?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it's not me, and I made it-- [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: --that I went and blow it up. There's no such a thing. This is-- you're talk-- we're talking cheap, you know what I mean? That's it-- that doesn't go that way. You have to accumulate. You have to make it. You have to do it. So I had a girl over there, from my city.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she brought the powder inside--

speaker: interviewee
text: [CRYING] How many of you--

speaker: interviewee
text: --the vagine, in the-- in the body. She brought the powder in. No, she didn't brought it so much like that another girl brought it. And she took it from them. And she gave it to me. And I made it-- a hand grenade. But that alone was no good. You couldn't build up-- blow up a building like that.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I like-- I was a welder. You know, what is a bomb? A bomb is nothing. A bomb is a bottle of oxygen or a bottle of acetylene-- is a bomb. Thank you. We're gonna take a little break right now.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's all right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.
